assesment:
Basal Cell Carcinoma - Superficial
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment: Mohs done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the procedure. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever.
Follow up: after 2 Weeks for Suture removal with Babar Rao, MD
mohsNotes:
Site A
Location: Left Chest (A)
Pre-op Diagnosis: Basal Cell Carcinoma Superficial
Pathology Reference: RA23-03224
Mohs Log: 24-06
Site B
 Location: Left Chest (B)
Pre-op Diagnosis: Basal Cell Carcinoma Superficial
Pathology Reference: RA23-03225
Mohs Log: 24-07
Allergies: No Known Allergies,
In an informative discussion of the mohs procedure, the nature of the skin cancer, 
 alternative treatment options, consequences of no treatment and convalescence were reviewed with the patient. 
 The risks and benefits, including but not limited to: bleeding, spread of infection, pain, scarring, bruising, 
 distortion of the surrounding areas, allergic reaction to the medications used, recurrence of the tumor, 
 and opening of the wound were discussed and understood by the patient. 
All questions from the patient were answered adequately. 
 No guarantees were given and the patient was informed that Mohs surgery is indicated to provide 
 the highest cure rate by excising the tissue in multiple stages and preserving the healthy tissue as much as possible. 
After obtaining informed consent, the patient was given a mirror and 
 corroborated the location of the tumor prior to surgery. 
 Mohs Micrographic Procedure:
Attention was turned toward the surgical site, which was disinfected and cleansed with hibiclens. The patient was draped using a sterile technique. Local anesthetic lidocaine 1 % with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. The procedure was performed in accordance with the Mohs micrographic surgical technique. 
 The tumor site was debulk prior to the first Mohs stage. With each stage, a thin layer of tissue was excised with a narrow margin of normal appearing skin using the Mohs fresh tissue technique. The surgical site was dressed with gauze pressure dressings and the patient remained in the surgical area. The tissue was prepared for the cryostat and sectioned. Each section was coded, cut and stained for microscopic examination. The slides were then analyzed by the Mohs surgeon for any evidence of remaining tumor. Areas noted to be positive on the previous stage (if applicable) were removed with the mohs technique and processed for analysis. This process was repeated until a tumor free plane was obtained.
 Site A
1st Stage: 2 Sections, Positive
2nd Stage: 1 Sections, Negative
Site B
1st Stage: 2 Sections, Positive
2nd Stage: 2 Sections, Positive
3rd Stage: 1 Sections, Negative
Post-Mohs Defect Size for both sites: 2.5 x 3.2 cm.
 Repair Options:
 
 After a discussion of wound management options, 
 due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, 
 avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result. 
 Performed: The wound for boths sites was closed with a single Complex Closure on the same day of mohs.
 
 Reconstruction:
Repair: Complex Closure
The postoperative deficit was 2.5 x 3.2 cm. The wound was closed with a complex closure. The defect was undermined extensively (8.0 cm) to reduce tension and distortion of the adjacent structures. Buried absorbable sutures were also used to close the subcutaneous and dermal components of the defect. The wound was approximated utilizing 3-O Vicryl and 3-O Nylon sutures.
 
The final closure size is 10.5 cm. 
 Post-Operative:
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications, and minimal bleeding. The wound was covered with Vaseline and Press Dressing. Detailed oral and written post-operative instructions were reviewed and given to the patient. The patient instructed to remove dressing in 24 hours, sleep with the surgical site elevated this evening, avoid alcohol, trauma and tobacco.
 Patient was informed to return in 2 Weeks.
examination: - well healed scar at site of previous biopsy located on the left chest, x2.

Squamous Cell Carcinoma In Situ
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, cryosurgery, PDT, and Imiquimod/5FU . Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment: Mohs done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the procedure. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever. After 24 hours, remove bandage and apply Mupirocin Ointment to wound site one to two times daily with bandage changes for 5 - 7 days.
Follow up: after 2 Weeks for Suture removal with Babar Rao, MD

mohsNotes:
Location: Left Hand
Pre-op Diagnosis: Squamous Cell Carinoma In Situ
Pathology Reference: RA23-03223
Mohs Log: 24-12
Allergies: No Known Allergies, 
Current Medication: Asprin
In an informative discussion of the mohs procedure, the nature of the skin cancer, 
 alternative treatment options, consequences of no treatment and convalescence were reviewed with the patient. 
 The risks and benefits, including but not limited to: bleeding, spread of infection, pain, scarring, bruising, 
 distortion of the surrounding areas, allergic reaction to the medications used, recurrence of the tumor, 
 and opening of the wound were discussed and understood by the patient. 
All questions from the patient were answered adequately. 
 No guarantees were given and the patient was informed that Mohs surgery is indicated to provide 
 the highest cure rate by excising the tissue in multiple stages and preserving the healthy tissue as much as possible. 
After obtaining informed consent, the patient was given a mirror and 
 corroborated the location of the tumor prior to surgery. 
 Mohs Micrographic Procedure:
Attention was turned toward the surgical site, which was disinfected and cleansed with hibiclens. The patient was draped using a sterile technique. Local anesthetic lidocaine 1 % with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. The procedure was performed in accordance with the Mohs micrographic surgical technique. 
 The tumor site was debulk prior to the first Mohs stage. With each stage, a thin layer of tissue was excised with a narrow margin of normal appearing skin using the Mohs fresh tissue technique. Hemostasis was achieved with Pressure. The surgical site was dressed with gauze pressure dressings and the patient remained in the surgical area. The tissue was prepared for the cryostat and sectioned. Each section was coded, cut and stained for microscopic examination. The slides were then analyzed by the Mohs surgeon for any evidence of remaining tumor. Areas noted to be positive on the previous stage (if applicable) were removed with the mohs technique and processed for analysis. This process was repeated until a tumor free plane was obtained. 
1st Stage: 1 Sections, Negative
Post-Mohs Deficit Size: 2.6 x 2.9 cm.
 Repair Options:
 
 After a discussion of wound management options, 
 due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, 
 avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result. 
 Performed: 
 The wound was closed with an Adjacent Tissue Transfer on the same day of mohs. 
 Reconstruction:
Repair: Adjacent Tissue Transfer - Advanced 
The postoperative deficit was 2.6 x 2.9 cm. Additional relaxing incisions were made along the wound to develop pedicle skin flaps which were then widely undermined and advanced to close the deficit. The defect was undermined extensively (1.6 cm) to reduce tension and distortion of the adjacent structures. Buried absorbable sutures were also used to close the subcutaneous and dermal components of the defect. The wound was approximated utilizing 4-O Vicryl and 4-O Nylon sutures. 
The final closure size is 4.2 cm2. 
 Post-Operative:
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications, and minimal bleeding. The wound was covered with Vaseline and Press Dressing. Detailed oral and written post-operative instructions were reviewed and given to the patient. The patient instructed to remove dressing in 24 hours, sleep with the surgical site elevated this evening, avoid alcohol, trauma and tobacco.
 Patient was informed to return in 2 Weeks.
examination: - well healed scar at site of previous biopsy located on the left hand.

Basal Cell Carcinoma-Nodular
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risks and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment:
 Mohs done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the procedure. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever.
 
 After 48 hours, remove bandage and apply Mupirocin Ointment to wound site two times daily with bandage changes for 7 days. 
 -Start Cephalexin-500mg tid, as directed. -Patient denies any allergies to cephalosporins. Discussed with patient that this medication is used to treat bacterial infections and should be taken with food at the same time daily to avoid upset stomach.  Generally, most feel better after the first few days of treatment however advised patient to finish the entire course of treatment. If treatment is stopped or doses are skipped, the infection may not be completely treat and the bacteria may become resistant to antibiotics. 
Follow up: after 2 Weeks for Suture removal with Babar Rao, MD

assesment:
Squamous Cell Carcinoma (MAD25-00851)
Considering the patients history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment:
Mohs done today. Discussed the procedure with the patient and answered all of their questions. The pathology report and previous biopsy photos were reviewed and the site was confirmed with the patient. Patient verbalized understanding of the risks of surgery, including infection and scarring. Patient opted to proceed with the procedure. Patient instructed to monitor for signs of infection, including swelling, erythema, wound drainage or fever
-Had an informative discussion about the Mohs procedure with the patient. Reviewed with patient that Mohs surgery is a procedure to treat skin cancer that involves cutting away thin layers of the skin while sacrificing a minimum amount of normal tissue. Mohs surgery is ideal for skin cancers that have a high risk of recurrence, for areas where one would want to keep as much healthy tissue as possible, for tumors that have edges that are hard to define or are large and grow quickly. Risks of Mohs surgery include but are not limited to: bleeding, pain or tenderness at site, and possible risk of infection and large scarring. Other uncommon risks are temporary or permanent numbness and/or weakness of the surgical area.
- Start topical Mupirocin 2% ointment apply to affected area TID. 
Actinic Keratosis
 Discussed with patient that these are considered to be precancerous and are primarily caused by long-term exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the sun but can be caused from other sources. Management options were discussed, including: cryotherapy, topical therapies (5FU, imiquimod, etc.). Risks and benefits of each reviewed, with the primary goal of treatment to prevent the progression of the lesion. Patient educated on the importance of sun protection.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment: 
 
- Will treat with cryo at f/u frontal scalp/ left cheek. Picture taken on scalp. 
Follow up: after 2 Weeks for Suture removal with Mahin Alamgir, MD
currentmedication:
- Efudex 5 % topical cream #40 Rx 3
- Aspirin Oral #0 Rx 0
- Atenolol
- Atorvastatin Oral #0 Rx 0
- Citilopram
- Losartan
mohsNotes:
Location: Left Scalp
Pre-op Diagnosis: Squamous Cell Carcinoma 
Pathology Reference: MAD25-00851
Mohs Log: 6408
In an informative discussion of the mohs procedure, the nature of the skin cancer, 
 alternative treatment options, consequences of no treatment and convalescence were reviewed with the patient. 
 The risks and benefits, including but not limited to: bleeding, spread of infection, pain, scarring, bruising, 
 distortion of the surrounding areas, allergic reaction to the medications used, recurrence of the tumor, 
 and opening of the wound were discussed and understood by the patient. 
All questions from the patient were answered adequately. 
 No guarantees were given and the patient was informed that Mohs surgery is indicated to provide 
 the highest cure rate by excising the tissue in multiple stages and preserving the healthy tissue as much as possible. 
After obtaining informed consent, the patient was given a mirror and 
 corroborated the location of the tumor prior to surgery. 
 Mohs Micrographic Procedure:
Attention was turned toward the surgical site, which was disinfected and cleansed with chlorhexidine. The patient was draped using a sterile technique. Local anesthetic lidocaine 1 % with epinephrine was infiltrated around the site. The procedure was performed in accordance with the Mohs micrographic surgical technique. 
 The tumor site was debulk prior to the first Mohs stage. With each stage, a thin layer of tissue was excised with a narrow margin of normal appearing skin using the Mohs fresh tissue technique. Hemostasis was achieved with Pressure, Electrocautery. The surgical site was dressed with gauze pressure dressings and the patient remained in the surgical area. The tissue was prepared for the cryostat and sectioned. Each section was coded, cut and stained for microscopic examination. The slides were then analyzed by the Mohs surgeon for any evidence of remaining tumor. Areas noted to be positive on the previous stage (if applicable) were removed with the mohs technique and processed for analysis. This process was repeated until a tumor free plane was obtained. 
1st Stage: 2 Sections, Negative
Post-Mohs Deficit Size: 1.2 x 1.5 cm.
 Repair Options:
 
 After a discussion of wound management options, 
 due to the size and location of the defect, and the need to achieve optimal cosmesis, 
 avoid hematoma, the following wound management was agreed upon to provide the best functional and cosmetic result. 
 Performed: 
 The wound was closed with a Layered Closure on the same day of mohs. 
 Reconstruction:
Repair: Layered Closure
 The wound was closed with an intermediate layered closure on 12-17-2025. The wound involved the dermis and subcutaneous tissue which required a layer repair to minimize tension and promote optimal healing. The closure was performed in two layers; the deep dermis/ subcutaneous layer was approximated utilizing 3-O Vicryl absorbable sutures while the epidermal layer was closed with the use of 3-O Nylon sutures. 
This layered closure was necessary due to the wounds depth and tissue separation. The location of the wound required reinforcement with a deep dermal closure to prevent breakdown and minimize risk of wound complications. The final closure size is 2.3 cm.
Pressure was applied to achieve hemostasis. Aquaphor was applied at the wound site and it was bandaged. Patient tolerated the procedure well and wound care instructions were reviewed and given to the patient.
NOTE: null 
 Post-Operative:
The patient tolerated the procedure well with no complications, and minimal bleeding. The wound was covered with Vaseline and Press Dressing. Detailed oral and written post-operative instructions were reviewed and given to the patient. The patient instructed to remove dressing in 24 hours, sleep with the surgical site elevated this evening, avoid alcohol, trauma and tobacco.
 Patient was informed to return in 2 Weeks.
examination:
- scaly, erythematous plaque located on left scalp
- erythematous, scaly, indurated papules located on; frontal scalp
